mappings.rationaleAI: As the overview article for the entire cluster of social science disciplines, it addresses the history and scope of anthropology, sociology, economics, political science, geography, psychology, and linguistics, providing broad coverage across all outline divisions.

Sociology traces its origins with Comte, Marx, and Durkheim (A.1, A.2) and 20th-century methodological evolution (A.3), and comprehensively covers the background, methodology, status, and emergent trends of sociology as well as cognate disciplines including criminology and demography (C.1–C.5).

Political science covers the discipline's emergence as an academic field in the late 19th century (E.1), the scope and quantitative methods of contemporary political science including comparative politics and formal modelling (E.2), and cognate areas such as public administration and international relations (E.3).

Economics covers the full history of economic thought from classical through Keynesian and post-2008 frameworks (D.1), the scope and methods including microeconomics and macroeconomics (D.2), and cognate disciplines including mathematical economics and behavioral economics (D.3).

Anthropology directly addresses B.1 (background of anthropology) and B.2 (scope and methods including the division between cultural/sociocultural and physical/biological anthropology), with ethnography as its core method and all four subfields covered.

Government covers constitutional frameworks, branches of power, and historical and contemporary forms of governance, mapping to the scope and methods of contemporary political science (E.2) and cognate areas including public administration and political systems (E.3).

Human migration is a core subject of demography — a cognate discipline explicitly listed under C.5 — and connects to human geography's study of population distribution and settlement patterns within F.2 (scope and methods of geography).

Marx is the primary source of Marxist influence on the social sciences (A.3.b), and his economic theory — historical materialism, surplus value, and critique of capitalism — was foundational to the development of economic theory (D.1), particularly as a challenge to classical political economy.

The history of philosophy traces the intellectual precursors from which the social sciences emerged, including Scholastic and Renaissance thought (A.1.a) and Enlightenment social and political philosophy (A.1.b) that directly shaped the reform movements from which social science developed.

Social class — covering Marxist, Weberian, and Bourdieusian stratification theory — is a foundational topic in the background and contemporary practice of sociology (C.1), connecting directly to sociology's study of social structure and the dynamics of stratification central to C.3 and C.4.

Human behavior covers the full interdisciplinary range of behavioral science touching the nature and scope of psychology (G.2), and its social dimensions — norms, conformity, group behavior — relate to social psychology as a cognate discipline of sociology (C.5).

Geography directly addresses F.1 (history of geography from Eratosthenes onward) and F.2 (scope and methods including the division between physical, human, and regional geography as well as technical/GIS approaches).

Statistics maps to A.3.d.iii (introduction of mathematical and quantitative methods and computers in social science) and A.3.d.iv (empiricism, data collection, surveys, and testing of theories); it also underlies econometrics as a cognate discipline of economics (D.3).

Emotion is a central topic within the nature and scope of psychology (G.2) and connects to physiological and social psychology as special branches (G.3), with the article covering neurophysiological substrates, evolutionary accounts, and cross-cultural variation.

The state article covers theories of state function, legitimacy, and historical forms of governance, directly relevant to the scope of contemporary political science (E.2) and the foundational political concept that defines the discipline's primary object of study.

Kant's political philosophy and Enlightenment epistemology were central to the heritage of the Enlightenment (A.1.b) that directly fostered conditions for social science's emergence through emphasis on rational, empirical reform of social and political institutions.

The Enlightenment is precisely identified in A.1.b as the heritage of social reforms and revolution from which social science emerged; its coverage of political theory, economics, and rational governance directly shaped the origins of the separate social science disciplines.

The Information Age article covers computing, digital data, and the transformation of knowledge production, mapping to A.3.d.iii's focus on the introduction of mathematical, computational, and quantitative methods and computer use in the social sciences.

Gender as a socioculturally constructed category is a key concern of sociocultural anthropology's scope and methods (B.2) and of sociology's study of social structure and stratification (C), with the article covering both social science and biological theoretical frameworks.

Society is the foundational object of sociological inquiry, and the article's coverage of social structure, roles, and theoretical frameworks — functionalism, conflict theory, symbolic interactionism — maps directly to the background and methodology of sociology (C.1, C.2).

Law is listed as a cognate area of political science through public law (E.3) and is connected to criminology and penology as cognate disciplines of sociology (C.5), making it substantively relevant at both of those intersections in the outline.

Machiavelli is conventionally identified as the founder of modern political science through his empirical analysis of power in The Prince and the Discourses, placing him as a formative figure in the history of political science (E.1).

Locke's social contract theory and natural rights doctrine were foundational to the Enlightenment heritage of political and social reform (A.1.b) and to the history of political science (E.1), directly influencing constitutional liberalism and later empirical social science.

Freudian ideas are explicitly named as a major 20th-century influence on the social sciences (A.3.c); Freud also anchors the history of psychology (G.1) and his psychoanalytic framework shaped the nature and scope of psychology (G.2) and social psychology as a cognate discipline.

Imperialism connects to international relations as a cognate area of political science (E.3), and the anti-imperialist revolution of rising expectations in the non-Western world is one of the named influences on 20th-century social science development (A.3.a).

Culture is the central object of study in sociocultural anthropology's scope and methods (B.2) and a fundamental concept in sociology's study of shared norms, institutions, and collective life (C), with the article covering both anthropological and sociological theoretical frameworks.

Ethnicity as a socioculturally constructed group identity is foundational in sociocultural anthropology (B.2 — cultural meaning, kinship, identity) and sociology (C — stratification, social structure), with the article covering primordialism, constructivism, and inter-ethnic dynamics.

Politics provides the overview of collective decision-making and power distribution that frames the scope of political science as a discipline (E, E.2), covering forms of government, the political spectrum, and the relationship between political theory and empirical political science.

Ibn Khaldun is a medieval precursor to sociology and political science through his empirical theory of social cohesion and dynastic cycles (A.1.a), and his proto-macroeconomic analysis of supply, demand, and taxation places him in the development of economic theories (D.1).

Adam Smith is the founder of classical political economy, directly anchoring the development of economic theories (D.1); his work is also the paradigmatic example of the separation of economics as a distinct discipline in the 19th century (A.2.b).

Philosophy as the parent discipline of all systematic inquiry provides the intellectual background and precursor tradition (A.1.a) from which the social sciences emerged, covering logic, epistemology, and political and moral philosophy as antecedents to empirical social science.

Trade is a core subject of economic theory covering comparative advantage, market mechanisms, and the history of exchange from ancient networks through modern globalization, connecting to the development of economic theories (D.1) and the scope of economics as a discipline (D).

Finance is a cognate discipline of economics that fits within D.3 (mathematical economics, econometrics, accounting), and its focus on investment, risk management, and capital allocation reflects the applied quantitative extensions of economic science.
